original_abstract:
We introduce MCTest, an open-access dataset designed to advance research in the machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling and information extraction have made significant progress, they often focus on narrow domains or specialized sub-tasks like relation extraction. In contrast, MCTest challenges machines to answer multiple-choice questions based on fictional stories, directly addressing the broader goal of open-domain reading comprehension. 

A multiple-choice format allows for an objective evaluation metric while still requiring sophisticated capabilities such as causal reasoning and synthesis. Because the stories are fictional, the answers must be derived from the text itself rather than from external knowledge bases. Furthermore, the content is curated to be accessible to a young child, ensuring the task remains focused on linguistic comprehension rather than vast reservoirs of world knowledge.

We describe a scalable crowdsourcing pipeline that allowed us to efficiently generate 500 stories and 2,000 questions. By implementing rigorous quality controls—including grammar screenings for workers and systematic grading for stories—we achieved a level of data quality comparable to expert-edited sets at a fraction of the cost. Through this open-domain yet carefully bounded dataset, we aim to provide a clear benchmark for progress in human-level text understanding, offering a task that remains challenging for contemporary state-of-the-art algorithms.
humanized_abstract:
We present MCTest, a large open data set designed to improve the research of machine comprehension of text. While there has been tremendous amount of work on semantic modeling and information extraction, much of it has been limited to very specific domains, or focused on sub-tasks such as relation extraction. There is a pressing need for more general, real-world evaluation benchmarks. MCTest includes hundreds of fictional stories for training and testing with thousands of machine comprehension questions. These questions are cast in the multiple choice format with four possible answers per question. Machines have to read the stories and choose correct answers from the given set of options.

This is a multiple choice task that evaluates the candidate’s ability to apply causal inference and text flow understanding. Although the stories are purely fictional, the correct answers must be inferred directly from the provided text (as opposed to being looked up in a knowledge base). Given that the passage is designed to be easily understood by a young child, the task is largely about interpreting language, and does not require a knowledge base of any kind.

We describe a scalable workflow that leveraged crowdsourcing to generate 500 stories and 2,000 questions in a matter of weeks. We enforced language proficiency requirements for workers and used advanced algorithms to accurately assess story quality based on evaluations from a subset of questions, all while maintaining high data quality at an order of magnitude lower cost than professional content creators. We hope to establish a gold standard for achieving reasonable progress toward the goal of fully understanding text, providing a non-trivial yet well-defined task that even the most advanced algorithms will have difficulty solving.

We present MCTest, a large open data set designed to improve the research of machine comprehension of text. While there has been tremendous amount of work on semantic modeling and information extraction, much of it has been limited to very specific domains, or focused on sub-tasks such as relation extraction. These questions are cast in the multiple choice format with four possible answers per question. Machines have to read the stories and choose correct answers from the given set of options.

This is a multiple choice task that evaluates the candidate’s ability to apply causal inference and text flow understanding. Although the stories are purely fictional, the correct answers must be inferred directly from the provided text (as opposed to being looked up in a knowledge base). Given that the passage is designed to be easily understood by a young child, the task is largely about interpreting language, and does not require a knowledge base of any kind.

We describe a scalable workflow that leveraged crowdsourcing to generate 500 stories and 2,000 questions in a matter of weeks. document.input:
We introduce MCTest, an open-access dataset designed to advance research in the machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling and information extraction have made significant progress, they often focus on narrow domains or specialized sub-tasks like relation extraction. In contrast, MCTest challenges machines to answer multiple-choice questions based on fictional stories, directly addressing the broader goal of open-domain reading comprehension. 

A multiple-choice format allows for an objective evaluation metric while still requiring sophisticated capabilities such as causal reasoning and synthesis. Because the stories are fictional, the answers must be derived from the text itself rather than from external knowledge bases. Furthermore, the content is curated to be accessible to a young child, ensuring the task remains focused on linguistic comprehension rather than vast reservoirs of world knowledge.

We describe a scalable crowdsourcing pipeline that allowed us to efficiently generate 500 stories and 2,000 questions. By implementing rigorous quality controls—including grammar screenings for workers and systematic grading for stories—we achieved a level of data quality comparable to expert-edited sets at a fraction of the cost. Through this open-domain yet carefully bounded dataset, we aim to provide a clear benchmark for progress in human-level text understanding, offering a task that remains challenging for contemporary state-of-the-art algorithms.
